prompt: As of June 2026, what is the state of the art in speculative decoding / inference acceleration for LLM serving? Cover and rank: EAGLE-3 and successors, Medusa, Hydra, lookahead decoding, self-speculative, draft-model vs draft-head approaches, and any 2025-2026 methods. Give measured speedups (tokens/sec, acceptance rate) and vLLM/TensorRT-LLM/SGLang support status. Cite papers (arXiv id + date) and repos. Then: for a platform serving small distilled models with vLLM, which speculative method gives the best latency win with least training cost, and how should a draft model be auto-trained from the same distillation data?
content:
**EAGLE-3 (and extensions like P-EAGLE) is the current state-of-the-art** among the listed approaches as of June 2026, delivering the highest reported speedups and acceptance rates while seeing broad production integration. It builds on the EAGLE series by shifting to direct token prediction with multi-layer feature fusion (via "training-time test"/TTT) and retaining dynamic draft trees from EAGLE-2.[[1]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.01840)[[2]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.01840v1)

### Key Methods Overview and Ranking
Here is a ranked summary (focusing on lossless methods where possible; speedups are typically vs. standard autoregressive decoding at batch size 1 unless noted; practical numbers vary by model size, workload, framework, and acceptance length τ):

- **EAGLE-3 (arXiv:2503.01840, Mar 2025; repo: SafeAILab/EAGLE)**: Top-ranked. Direct token prediction + multi-layer (low/mid/high) feature fusion from the target model + dynamic/context-aware draft trees + TTT training. Paper reports ~3.0–6.5× speedup (e.g., up to ~4.8–5.6× on Llama/Vicuna 13B/70B-class models; ~1.4× over EAGLE-2). High acceptance rates (often enabling mean acceptance lengths of ~2–3+ tokens; real-world ~60–80%+ in tuned cases). Practical vLLM/SGLang gains: 1.5–2.5×+ latency/throughput (e.g., up to 2.5× in vLLM reports; higher on coding/reasoning). P-EAGLE (parallel drafting variant, integrated in vLLM ~early 2026) adds up to 1.69× over vanilla EAGLE-3.[[3]](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/p-eagle-faster-llm-inference-with-parallel-speculative-decoding-in-vllm/)[[4]](https://huggingface.co/blog/lujangusface/tw-eagle3-gpu)[[5]](https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2025/07/01/fly-eagle3-fly-faster-inference-vllm-speculative-decoding)[[5]](https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2025/07/01/fly-eagle3-fly-faster-inference-vllm-speculative-decoding)[[2]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.01840v1)

  **Support**: Native/full in vLLM (v0.8.5+, with CUDA graphs, acceptance metrics like mean acceptance length; EAGLE-3 checkpoints via `--speculative-model`), SGLang (`speculative_algorithm: EAGLE3`), and TensorRT-LLM (Eagle3 decoding_type). Widely used in production.

- **EAGLE / EAGLE-2 (EAGLE: arXiv:2401.15077, Jan 2024; EAGLE-2: arXiv:2406.16858, Jun 2024; same repo)**: Strong runners-up. Feature-level autoregression (EAGLE) + dynamic trees (EAGLE-2). Speedups ~2–4×+ (EAGLE-2 competitive but below EAGLE-3). High acceptance; lower overhead than separate draft models.[[6]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.15077)[[7]](https://github.com/SafeAILab/EAGLE)

  **Support**: Excellent across vLLM, SGLang, TRT-LLM.

- **Hydra (arXiv:2402.05109, ~Feb 2024; repo: zankner/Hydra)**: Draft-head evolution of Medusa with sequentially-dependent heads + tuned objectives/architecture (Hydra++). Up to 2.7× vs. autoregressive and 1.31× vs. Medusa. Good acceptance improvements via dependence modeling.[[8]](https://openreview.net/forum?id=FbhjirzvJG)[[9]](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.05109v2)

  **Support**: Integrated in frameworks supporting Medusa-style heads (vLLM etc.); less emphasis than EAGLE-3.

- **Medusa (arXiv:2401.10774, Jan 2024)**: Multiple parallel decoding heads (draft-head approach) on target hidden states + tree attention. Typical ~2× speedups (sometimes higher with tuning). Simpler than full draft models.[[2]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.01840v1)[[10]](https://www.together.ai/blog/medusa)

  **Support**: Broad (vLLM, etc.), predates EAGLE-3.

- **Lookahead decoding (arXiv:2402.02057, Feb 2024; repo: hao-ai-lab/LookaheadDecoding)**: No auxiliary model/data; parallel n-gram extraction/verification branches within the target model. Up to 1.8× on MT-Bench; stronger multi-GPU scaling (up to 4× in code tasks). Lower peak speedup than EAGLE but zero extra training.[[11]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.02057)

  **Support**: TensorRT-LLM (dedicated implementation); limited elsewhere.

- **Standard draft-model speculative sampling** (e.g., Leviathan et al. 2023 baseline): Separate small draft model proposes tokens, target verifies in parallel/tree attention. Speedups ~1.3–2.7× depending on draft quality/acceptance. Highest training/inference overhead.[[12]](https://nvidia.github.io/TensorRT-LLM/advanced/speculative-decoding.html)

- **Self-speculative / other draft-head variants** (e.g., Beagle/Budget EAGLE arXiv ~2505.24544 May 2025; DVI ~2025–2026): Lightweight heads or cross-attention on target features. Competitive with or slightly below EAGLE-3; emphasize low overhead.[[13]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.24544v3)

**2025–2026 extensions/competitors**: P-EAGLE (parallel EAGLE in vLLM), Meta-scale EAGLE optimizations (online distillation + longer training for Llama; tokens-per-call/TPC up to ~2.9), Mixture of Attentions (~9.5% over EAGLE-2), SpecForge (EAGLE-3 training framework), and training-aware methods like DVI (competitive speedups with far less data).[[14]](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.08192v1)[[15]](https://openreview.net/forum?id=CwvY6TXLxr)

**Draft-model vs. draft-head**: Draft-head approaches (EAGLE series, Medusa/Hydra) dominate for efficiency—lightweight (often 1–few layers/heads using target features), lower memory/training cost, and seamless integration (no separate tokenizer/model loading). Pure draft models offer flexibility but higher overhead. EAGLE-3 hybrids the best of both via feature reuse.

**Overall ranking for speed/acceptance (2026)**: EAGLE-3/P-EAGLE ≫ EAGLE-2/Hydra/Medusa ≫ Lookahead/draft-model (vanilla) ≫ basic self-speculative. Real-world factors (workload, batch size, tuning) matter; EAGLE-3 leads benchmarks and deployments.

### Recommendation for Small Distilled Models on vLLM
For platforms serving **small distilled models** with vLLM, **EAGLE-3 (draft-head)** provides the best latency win with the least training cost. It outperforms Medusa/Hydra and draft-model approaches in acceptance rate/speedup while requiring only a lightweight head (not a full separate draft model), minimizing extra parameters and training data/compute. vLLM has mature native support (including metrics and graphs), and gains are often 1.5–2.5×+ in practice with proper tuning.[[5]](https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2025/07/01/fly-eagle3-fly-faster-inference-vllm-speculative-decoding)[[16]](https://www.spheron.network/blog/eagle-3-speculative-decoding-gpu-cloud/)

**Auto-training a draft model/head from the same distillation data**:
- Use the distillation dataset (or trajectories generated by the target/distilled model on it) to train the EAGLE-3-style head.
- Leverage TTT (training-time test) simulation during training to mimic multi-step drafting and improve long-horizon accuracy (key EAGLE-3 innovation).
- Incorporate multi-layer feature fusion (extract hidden states from target layers) and dynamic tree elements if scaling training.
- Tools like SpecForge or vLLM’s speculator training support (v0.3+) facilitate this; fine-tune on target-generated samples for domain alignment. Online distillation or extended training (as in Meta Llama optimizations) further boosts robustness.[[17]](https://vllm.ai/blog/2025-12-13-speculators-v030)[[18]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.18567v1)

This keeps overhead minimal while maximizing vLLM compatibility and acceptance rates. Test acceptance length and end-to-end latency on your workloads, as real gains depend on data similarity. Pre-trained EAGLE-3 checkpoints exist for popular bases and can be adapted.
